title: Create your weights profile
text: risk.assessr package default weighting profile assigns weighting profile 1 riskmetric giving equal weighting risk metric. wish create weighting profile, 1 - go R script create_weights_profile() R folder. 2 - Open script see following code: 3 - set weights risk metric 4 - save file 5 - Go Build Tab 6 - Click Tab 7 - Click Document, Clean Install, Load 8 - Run riskmetric functions assess_pkg() assess_pkg_r_package() calculate riskmetrics using weights profile.
code: ``` r library(risk.assessr)  create_weights_profile <- function() {   weights <- c(     has_bug_reports_url = .2,     license = .1,     has_examples = .4,     has_maintainer = .2,     has_news = .2,     size_codebase = .2,     has_source_control = .1,     has_vignettes = .4,     has_website = .4,     news_current = .2,     export_help = .2,     export_calc = .2,     check = .75,     covr = .6,     dep_score = .5,     revdep_score = .1   )   return(weights) }

title: Assessing package risk metrics
text: Open-source projects can improve clinical trials work making transparent collaborative solve problems define common standard clinical trial ecosystem. Successful open source packages admiral, tern well collaborative initiatives (Pharmaverse, Transcellerate, Phuse) greatly standardize harmonize processes different laboratories well allowing faster deliverable submission. However, open source raises inherent challenges terms : need use open-source projects responsibly safely adhering best practices ensure reliability package utilized FDA submission. goal establish comprehensive reliable package helps determine package’s reliability security terms maintenance, documentation, dependencies. package designed carry risk assessment internal open source R packages. packages can: Actively run R CMD check (series technical checks ensure examples run successfully, tests pass, packages compatible packages CRAN) Provide answers certain steps passing Actively run test coverage checks ensure tests pass calculate unit test coverage create traceability matrix matches function / test descriptions tests match test pass/fail associated test coverage Can executed several environments (different OS, wise) different R versions Can work package versions Provide overall risk score calculates risk metrics : Core metrics - includes R command check, unit test coverage composite coverage dependencies Documentation metrics - availability vignettes, news tracking, example(s), return object description exported functions, type license

title: Assessing package risk metrics
text: package following steps workflow assess risk R package using riskmetric: Finding source package information locally (renv.lock file installed local package tar.gz file Assessing package validation criteria scoring assessment criteria assess_pkg() results assembled dataset validation criteria containing overall risk score package shown example . package executes following tasks: upload source package(tar.gz file) locally Unpack tar.gz file Install package locally Run code coverage Run traceability matrix Run R CMD check Run risk assessment metrics using default weighting

title: Assessing package risk metrics
Assessing package risk metrics
text: assess package, following steps: 1 - save package tar.gz file can done RStudio -> Build Tab -> -> Build Source Package 2 - Run following code sample use file.choose load tar.gz file assess Open source package CRAN/Bioconductor
code: library(risk.assessr)  # for local tar.gz R package risk_assess_package <- risk.assessr::risk_assess_pkg() library(risk.assessr) # The function will retrieve the latest version of the package if no version is provided  results <- sanofi.risk.assessr::assess_pkg_r_package(package_name, version=NA)

title: Assess package — assess_pkg
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(risk.assessr) # set CRAN repo to enable running of reverse dependencies r = getOption("repos") r["CRAN"] = "https://cloud.r-project.org" options(repos = r)  pkg_source_path <- file.choose() pkg_name <- sub("\\.tar\\.gz$", "", basename(pkg_source_path))  modified_tar_file <- modify_description_file(pkg_source_path)  # Set up the package using the temporary file install_list <- risk.assessr::set_up_pkg(modified_tar_file)  # Extract information from the installation list build_vignettes <- install_list$build_vignettes package_installed <- install_list$package_installed pkg_source_path <- install_list$pkg_source_path rcmdcheck_args <- install_list$rcmdcheck_args  # check if the package needs to be installed locally package_installed <-    risk.assessr::install_package_local(pkg_source_path)  # Check if the package was installed successfully if (package_installed == TRUE) {   # Assess the package   assess_package <- risk.assessr::assess_pkg(pkg_source_path, rcmdcheck_args)   # Output the assessment result } else {   message("Package installation failed.") } } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   modified_tar <- modify_description_file("path/to/mypackage.tar.gz")   print(modified_tar)   # Unpack the tar.gz file   contents <- risk.assessr::unpack_tarball(modified_tar)   description_path <- file.path(contents, "DESCRIPTION")   description_content <- readLines(description_path)   cat(description_content, sep = "\n") } # }

text: pkg_source_path directory path R project rcmdcheck_args list arguments pass `rcmdcheck::rcmdcheck`

title: Run R CMD CHECK — run_rcmdcheck
text: rcmdcheck takes either tarball installation directory. basename `pkg_source_path` package name version pasted together returned score calculated via weighted sum notes (0.10), warnings (0.25), errors (1.0). maximum score 1 (indicating errors, notes warnings) minimum score 0 (equal 1 error, 4 warnings, 10 notes). scoring methodology taken directly [riskmetric::metric_score.pkg_metric_r_cmd_check()].

what: Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
title: Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
text: Calculates regularized score based number dependencies package . Convert number dependencies NROW(x) validation score [0,1] $$ 1 / (1 + exp(-0.5 * (NROW(x) + 19))) $$

title: Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
text: x number dependencies

title: Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
text: numeric value 0 (low number  dependencies)   1 (high number dependencies)

title: Score a package for dependencies — score_dependencies
text: removed - 1 formula e.g. $$ 1 - 1 / (1 + exp(NROW(x)- 19)) $$ reverse return value e.g. 0 - low; 1 - high scoring function classic logistic curve $$ / (1 + exp(-k(x-x[0])) $$ \(x = NROW(x)\), sigmoid midpoint 20 reverse dependencies, ie. \(x[0] = 19\), logistic growth rate \(k = 0.5\). $$ 1 / (1 + exp(NROW(x)- 19)) $$

what: Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
title: Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
text: Score package number reverse dependencies ; regularized Convert number reverse dependencies length(x) validation score [0,1] $$ 1 / (1 + exp(-0.5 * (sqrt(length(x)) + sqrt(20)))) $$

title: Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
text: x number dependencies

title: Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
text: numeric value 1 (high number reverse dependencies)   0 (low number reverse dependencies)

title: Scoring method for number of reverse dependencies a package has — score_reverse_dependencies
text: scoring function classic logistic curve $$ 1 / (1 + exp(-k(x-x[0])) $$ square root scale number reverse dependencies \(x = sqrt(length(x))\), sigmoid midpoint 20 reverse dependencies, ie. \(x[0] = sqrt(15)\), logistic growth rate \(k = 0.5\). $$ 1 / (1 + -0.5 * exp(sqrt(length(x)) - sqrt(20))) $$
